variables.content: For a homogeneous, fully spin-polarized strictly two-dimensional electron system with interaction-strength parameter r_s = (πn_{2D}a_B^2)^{-1/2} (where a_B = ħ^2κ/(m_b e^2) is the effective Bohr radius in the semiconductor), numerical evaluation of the self-energy formulas given above using a static charge local-field factor G_{\uparrow}^{+}(q) (supplied, in the calculations reported here, by the Moreno–Marinescu parameterization of G^{+}(q,ζ=1)) yields the Fermi-contour quasiparticle effective mass m_{\uparrow}^{*}(k_F^{\uparrow}) that is suppressed below the band mass m_b in the strong-coupling regime: specifically, for r_s ≳ 3 the many-body effective mass decreases with increasing r_s and falls below m_b.  This trend for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) case contrasts with the typical behavior in spin-unpolarized (paramagnetic) two-dimensional electron systems, where many calculations and experiments report m^{*} increasing with r_s in the dilute, interacting range.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolation of finite-N DMC quartic-fit effective masses yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.70(3) a.u., where m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} for the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.

For an ideal two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas with parabolic single-particle dispersion $\xi_{k}^{s}=(k^{2}-(k_{F}^{s})^{2})/2m$, bare Coulomb interaction $v_{q}=2\pi e^{2}/q$, and spin polarization $\zeta\in[0,1]$, the quasiparticle effective mass computed from the single-loop dynamical-screening self-energy given above and the on-shell mass formula exhibits a nontrivial dependence on both the density parameter $r_{s}$ and $\zeta$: the majority-spin (spin-up, $\uparrow$) effective mass $m_{\uparrow}^{*}$ decreases with increasing $\zeta$, with a weak decrease for small $\zeta$ and a much stronger decrease as $\zeta\to 1$, while the minority-spin (spin-down, $\downarrow$) effective mass $m_{\downarrow}^{*}$ is non-monotonic in $\zeta$, first increasing for small $\zeta$ and then sharply decreasing as $\zeta\to 1$.  Here $r_{s}$ is the two-dimensional Wigner–Seitz radius defined by $r_{s}\equiv m e^{2}/\sqrt{\pi n}$.  Quantitatively, at $r_{s}=5$ the majority-spin effective-mass renormalization $m_{\uparrow}^{*}/m$ differs by more than a factor of two between the unpolarized state ($\zeta=0$) and the fully polarized state ($\zeta=1$).  These results were obtained for $r_{s}$ values in the range $1\le r_{s}\le 10$ and are presented as $m^{*}/m$ versus $\zeta$ curves for $r_{s}=1,\dots,10$.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass obtained by extrapolating finite-N DMC quartic-fit results is m^*(∞)=0.74(1) a.u., using m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.

For the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.851(5) a.u.; here m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector appropriate to the fully spin-polarized system.

The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

For a two-dimensional electron gas with densities n_{s} in the range 3×10^{12} to 5×10^{12} cm^{-2} and an electron effective mass typical of silicon inversion layers, the electronic Fermi energy E_{\mathrm{F}} is sufficiently large that application of laboratory magnetic fields up to several tesla (for example, B\approx 5–7 T) produces negligible thermodynamic spin polarization of the free two-dimensional electron ensemble (i.e., the fraction of spin-polarized two-dimensional electrons remains small), so that field-induced changes in radiative intensity attributable to bulk two-dimensional-electron spin polarization are minimal.

Let $g$ denote the effective Landé g-factor (dimensionless), let $\mu_B$ denote the Bohr magneton, and let $E_p(r_s)$ and $E_u(r_s)$ denote the zero-temperature many-body free energy per carrier of the fully spin-polarized and of the unpolarized homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas respectively at the same density parameter $r_s$; the critical parallel magnetic field $H_c$ required to convert the ground state from unpolarized to fully spin polarized is estimated by equating the Zeeman energy gain per carrier $g\mu_B H_c$ to the many-body free-energy difference per carrier $E_p(r_s)-E_u(r_s)$, i.e. by solving
      $$
      H_c \;=\; \frac{E_p(r_s)-E_u(r_s)}{g\mu_B},
      $$
      neglecting orbital coupling to the field and finite-temperature effects; using DQMC free-energy differences computed for the homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas and adopting an effective product $(g\sigma_z)=1.1$ (where $\sigma_z$ is the average spin projection per carrier, with $\sigma_z=1$ for full polarization) appropriate to GaAs yields an estimate $H_c\simeq 0.7\,$T at $r_s=9$, and indicates that parallel fields of order $1\,$T suffice to fully polarize the ground state at the carrier densities considered.

Define the effective Landé g-factor $g$ (dimensionless) and the Bohr magneton $\mu_B$; define $E_p(r_s)$ and $E_u(r_s)$ as the many-body ground-state free energy per electron of a homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas in the fully spin-polarized and unpolarized states respectively, both evaluated at the same density parameter $r_s$. The critical parallel magnetic field $H_c$ required to convert the ground state from unpolarized to fully spin polarized is estimated by equating the single-electron Zeeman energy gain per electron $E_Z(g,H)=g\mu_B H$ to the many-body free-energy difference per electron $E_p(r_s)-E_u(r_s)$ and solving
      $$
      H_c \;=\; \frac{E_p(r_s)-E_u(r_s)}{g\mu_B},
      $$
      while neglecting orbital coupling to the field, collective many-body redistribution of energy beyond the per-electron free-energy difference, and finite-temperature effects.
      Francesco Rapisarda and Gaetano Senatore, Aust J. Phys. 49, 161 (1996)

For the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the first three angular-momentum Fermi liquid parameters divided by the quasiparticle effective mass were obtained by computing the single-spin Fourier projection a_l^{\uparrow\uparrow} = \tfrac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} N f_{\uparrow\uparrow}(\theta)\cos(l\theta)\,d\theta and forming F_l/m^{*} = \tfrac{r_s^2}{4} a_l^{\uparrow\uparrow}, then extrapolating the finite-size estimates versus particle number N with a power-law N^{-1/4} dependence to the thermodynamic limit. The thermodynamic-limit estimates (with statistical uncertainties from the extrapolation fits) are: at r_s = 1, F_0/m^{*} = -0.50(4), F_1/m^{*} = -0.22(3), F_2/m^{*} = -0.14(3); at r_s = 5, F_0/m^{*} = -1.5(2), F_1/m^{*} = -0.61(6), F_2/m^{*} = -0.13(6); and at r_s = 10, F_0/m^{*} = -1.8(3), F_1/m^{*} = -1.03(3), F_2/m^{*} = 0.08(8).
